preamble: The transformation from a voiceless uvular plosive [q] to a voiced velar fricative [ɣ] involves both spirantization and voicing. This shift is common in various dialects where uvular stops are weakened and voiced in intervocalic or final positions.

examples.note: In many modern Persian dialects, the classical uvular stop [q] has merged with the voiced velar fricative [ɣ].
Certain rural dialects of Arabic exhibit the spirantization of the qaf [q] to a voiced fricative [ɣ].

sources: Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
Watson, J. C. (2002). The Phonology and Morphology of Arabic.
